text:
Existing animal models and static cell cultures do not adequately replicate the human intestinal microenvironment and, therefore, the mechanism of mercury ion (Hg(II)) transport remains poorly understood. In this work, we demonstrated a gut-on-a-chip platform that replicates the in vivo intestine environment while providing simultaneous measurements of TEER and heavy metal uptake using integrated electrochemical sensors.

Our gut bioreactor platform mimics the in vivo environment of the small intestine. The gut bioreactor device is mounted with intestinal tissues, which are subjected to physiological fluid shear stress (0.02 dyne/cm²) and cyclic mechanical strain (1%, 0.15 Hz). After 48h, in-situ strain promotes the formation of tight physical barrier that exhibits characteristics of an intestinal absorptive barrier whilst promoting spontaneous growth of intestinal villi-like structures. Through using our sensor array, the distribution and kinetic of Hg(II) uptake is observed in real-time at different concentrations and the consequent damage to the cellular structure is recorded. Interestingly, the influence of mechanical force on the intestinal absorption of Hg(II) was demonstrated, in which the increase in tensile strain from 1% to 5% results in an increase of 23.59% in the amount of Hg(II) absorbed by the intestinal tissues. Furthermore, it is found that the up-regulated expression of the mechanosensitive channel Piezo1 and the metal transporter DMT1 are associated with mercury ion uptake, thereby suggesting the mechanical biochemistry interaction in intestinal absorption of heavy metals. Our gut bioreactor platform could be utilized as a high-throughput tool in toxicology studies and in unraveling human-specific transport pathways.
explanation: The passage is coherent and contains plausible, precise experimental details (e.g., specific shear stress and % strain). While this can reflect genuine research, such polished, detail-rich text without citations can also be AI-generated.
